cards.title: What is an LLM Agent?
cards.teaser: An LLM by itself just predicts text. Wrap it in a loop that can call tools and react to results, and you have an agent.

runs.text: A standalone language model is a single-pass token generator. You give it a prompt, it returns a completion, and the conversation ends. Useful — but confined to whatever it learned during training.
An LLM
agent

embeds the same model in a loop that can act on the world. The agent reasons about the next step, calls a tool to do something, reads the tool's output back into context, and decides again. That feedback loop is what separates the two.

items.text: — the model proposes the next action.
— a tool runs (search, code, an API call).
— the result re-enters the prompt as new context.
— the next reasoning step accounts for the new evidence.

runs.text: Like the difference between a junior dev locked in a room with a notebook and one with Stack Overflow, an IDE, and a debugger. Same person in the seat — the tools and the feedback loop are what produce the work.

cards.title: Reason → Act → Observe
cards.teaser: The basic agent cycle. Three steps that, looped, turn a static model into something that adapts.

runs.text: Every agent reduces to the same three-step cycle, repeated until the goal is met or a stopping condition fires.

items.text: — given the current state, decide what to do next.
— invoke a tool, run code, query an API.
— read the result back into context.

runs.text: The loop continues from there. The model's
next
decision is informed by the
previous
action's outcome. Without the observe step, you have a chatbot. With it, you have something that can correct itself.
Like a chef tasting a dish, adjusting the seasoning, and tasting again. Each loop is small; the
sequence
of loops is where intelligence shows up.

cards.title: Stateless vs Stateful
cards.teaser: Whether the model remembers anything between calls is the line between using an LLM and building an agent.

runs.text: A
stateless
call treats the model as a black box: prompt in, completion out, nothing remembered. Run it twice with the same prompt and you get two independent answers.

explanation.code:
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-5-mini")
response = llm.invoke("What are AI agents?")
print(response.content)

runs.text: A
stateful
workflow keeps a structured memory across turns: messages, tool results, intermediate plans. Each new step starts from where the last one left off.
In LangGraph, state is just a TypedDict that nodes read from and write to. Plain Python — no magic.
Stateless is a REST endpoint — every request stands alone. Stateful is a session — the server remembers who you are between requests.

cards.title: Finite State Machine
cards.teaser: The base paradigm of agent control flow. A small vocabulary that scales surprisingly far before you need anything fancier.

runs.text: A finite state machine (FSM) models a system as a small set of named states and the moves between them. The same pattern you've already seen in compilers, network protocols, and form wizards — applied to agent reasoning.

items.text: — a snapshot of what the agent knows right now (messages, progress markers).
— something that happened since the last decision (tool returned, user replied).
— a check that decides which transition to take.
— work performed during the transition (call a tool, save a checkpoint).
— the condition that ends the run.

runs.text: FSMs shine when behavior alternates between a few stable modes and you care about being able to resume after a crash — every state is a natural checkpoint.
Like a checkout flow: cart → address → payment → review. Each step has guards (
"is the address valid?"
) and actions (
"charge the card"
). Crash at "payment" and you can resume there, not back at "cart".

cards.title: Hierarchical State Machine
cards.teaser: An FSM whose states can themselves contain states. Solves the spaghetti problem you hit when an FSM grows past a handful of nodes.

runs.text: Once you add planning, reflection, retries, and approval gates, a flat FSM either duplicates logic across edges or becomes a tangle of routes. Hierarchical state machines (HSMs) fix this by letting states
contain
other states.

items.text: — wraps a group of substates, defining shared entry/exit logic and policies.
— a concrete mode that lives inside a superstate and inherits its guards.
so resuming the superstate lands at the right substate.
— child regions that advance independently and rejoin later.

runs.text: In agent terms, a
WORKING
superstate might contain
PLAN
ACT
, and
REFLECT
substates. Rate limits or safety filters defined on the superstate apply to all three for free.
Like nesting folders. The parent folder's permissions apply to everything inside it; you don't re-set them on every file.

cards.title: State Schema
cards.teaser: A typed dict that defines the bus every node reads and writes. The shared memory of an agent graph.

runs.text: In LangGraph, the state schema is just a Python
TypedDict
(or Pydantic model). It declares the keys nodes are allowed to mutate — typically a message list and any progress markers.
The
Annotated[..., add_messages]
is LangGraph's reducer pattern: when two nodes both write
messages
, they're appended rather than overwriting. Custom reducers handle merging for any field.
Keep the schema small. Anything not in the schema can't be persisted by the checkpointer — and anything that
is
in the schema is read by every node, so it pays to be precise.
Like a JSON request body that every middleware can read and mutate before the response is sent. The schema is the contract.

cards.title: Nodes and Edges
cards.teaser: LangGraph's two primitives. Nodes do work; edges decide what runs next.

runs.text: A
node
is a function from state to state. It takes the current snapshot, performs reasoning or a side effect, and returns the keys it changed. The framework merges your return into the shared state.
An
edge
wires the graph. Plain edges always fire (
A → B
); conditional edges run a small router function and return the name of the next node.
Nodes stay deterministic in shape — the LLM call inside is non-deterministic, but the
graph topology
is fixed. That's what makes the system auditable.
Nodes are React components — each owns a piece of work. Edges are the router — they decide which screen renders next given the URL (state).

cards.title: Tools
cards.teaser: How an LLM acts on the world. A tool is just a typed function exposed to the model with a description it can read.

runs.text: In LangChain, you mark a function with
@tool
and the framework builds the schema the model sees from the function's name, signature, and docstring. The docstring is
not
optional — it's the description the LLM uses to decide whether to call this tool at all.
A vague docstring produces invalid tool calls. Treat it like an API description, not a code comment.
Like exporting a function from a worker thread. The docstring is the type signature the consumer reads — get it wrong and they call you wrong.

cards.title: The Tool-Calling Loop
cards.teaser: The minimum viable agent — a `while` loop that lets the model call tools until it stops asking.

runs.text: Before reaching for LangGraph, you can write a stateless agent in ~30 lines. The shape is always the same:

items.text: Send the conversation to the model.
, run each tool and append the results.
so a stuck model doesn't burn your budget.

runs.text: Like polling a long-running job until status is
done
. The trick is the timeout — without
max_steps
, a confused model will loop forever.

cards.title: Memory and Checkpointing
cards.teaser: Save state at every node so the agent can resume — and use thread IDs to keep parallel conversations isolated.

runs.text: A
checkpointer
persists the graph's state after each node runs. On resume, the framework rebuilds the conversation from the last checkpoint. LangGraph's
MemorySaver
does this in-process; production setups use Postgres or Redis.

explanation.code:
checkpointer = MemorySaver()
app = graph.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer)

cfg = {"configurable": {"thread_id": "session-1"}}
app.invoke({"messages": [HumanMessage("start")]}, config=cfg)

runs.text: The
thread_id

is the conversation's identity. Same id → continues the same memory. Different id → branches a new conversation that doesn't see the old one. Switching threads is how you let users explore alternatives without contaminating the main path.
Like git branches against the same repo.
thread_id
picks the branch; the checkpointer is the object database that holds every commit.

cards.title: Multi-Agent System (MAS)
cards.teaser: When one agent's responsibilities grow too wide, split the work across specialized agents under a supervisor.

runs.text: A single contributor can build the whole product. Past a certain size, you split into backend / frontend / QA and hire a tech lead. Multi-agent systems mirror this exactly: a supervisor agent routes work to specialized sub-agents.

items.text: — picks which sub-agent handles the next step.
— each owns a narrow domain (search, planning, code-writing).
— the message bus all of them read and write.

runs.text: Architecturally this is just an HSM: the supervisor is the superstate, specialists are substates. Shared policies (rate limits, safety filters) live on the superstate and apply to all of them.
Like a small dev team. You start as a solo IC. You hit a complexity wall, hire specialists, then realize someone has to coordinate them — that's the supervisor agent.

cards.title: Orchestrated Autonomy
cards.teaser: Today's agents don't redesign their own control graph. They make choices inside a graph you wrote.

runs.text: It's tempting to imagine agents as a
deus ex machina
— a god from the machine that solves your problem with no plumbing. That's not what's deployable today. Real agents are engineered systems with bounded autonomy.

items.text: — pick which tool to call, decide if more steps are needed, branch between predefined paths, write small bits of code to determine next steps.
— generate new graph topologies on the fly, invent new tools, redefine their own architecture.

runs.text: The autonomy spectrum runs from a single-guard router (one decision) → tool-calling agent (multi-step reasoning) → MAS (specialists under a supervisor). Almost every shipped system lives in the middle of that spectrum.
Like Tesla autopilot. It can change lanes, brake, follow the road you're on. It can't decide where you're going. The framework is yours; the choices inside it are the agent's.

cards.title: Why Constraints Are Features
cards.teaser: Agents are useful *because* they're bounded. Strip the constraints and you get something that won't ship.

runs.text: Humans don't operate with unconstrained freedom either. Coding standards, project requirements, and code review all bound a developer's choices — and that structure is what makes their output trustworthy.

Agents inherit the same trade-off. The tools you give them, the safeguards you enforce, and the workflow you allow them to operate inside are what make the system safe to deploy. A maximally autonomous agent is also maximally unpredictable — and unpredictability is what gets pulled from production.
The AI Scientist v2 had a paper accepted at an ICLR workshop in 2025. Two of its three submissions were rejected, and even the accepted one had methodological gaps. The bounded version that
also
fails on rigor is closer to reality than either the hype or the doomer take.
Constraints are guard rails on the highway. They don't slow you down — they're the only reason you can drive 70.
